coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) enthused the inquisitiveness of investigators in biomedicine due to their distinctive features including minimal toxicity, superior water solubility and biocompatibility. This study uses an environmental friendly hydrothermal approach to create high-fluorescent agricultural residue-derived carbon quantum dots from Soybean Oil Cake (SOC). The synthesized SOCC-Dots were examined by physiochemical techniques such as High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM), UV–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Photoluminescence (PL), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), Zeta potential and Raman Spectroscopy. The absorption peak at 275 nm is evident due to the presence of C = O bonds of π→π* transition. The synthesized SOCC-Dots demonstrated the existence of several functional groups OH, C = O, and CO stretching vibration. The particles collected display spherical morphology with an average of 3.25 nm size by HR-TEM analysis spectra. The photoluminescence showed stability against various excitation wavelengths and had a maximum emission peak at 445 nm. Anti-inflammatory activity is tested using a Bovine serum assay displaying significant results of 87.4 % for SOCC-dots at a concentration of 50 µg/mL due to their superior biocompatibility and water solubility. The results of the cytotoxicity evaluation showed an 85 % survival rate among nauplii after 48 h at concentrations of 80 µg/mL, and the antioxidant activity exhibited outstanding performance, achieving an 87 % efficacy rate for SOCC-dots at a concentration of 50 μg/mL

head.ce:title: Exploration of value-added soybean oil cake based carbon quantum dots for in-vitro biomedical applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) enthused the inquisitiveness of investigators in biomedicine due to their distinctive features including minimal toxicity, superior water solubility and biocompatibility. This study uses an environmental friendly hydrothermal approach to create high-fluorescent agricultural residue-derived carbon quantum dots from Soybean Oil Cake (SOC). The synthesized SOCC-Dots were examined by physiochemical techniques such as High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM), UV–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Photoluminescence (PL), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), Zeta potential and Raman Spectroscopy. The absorption peak at 275 nm is evident due to the presence of C = O bonds of π→π* transition. The synthesized SOCC-Dots demonstrated the existence of several functional groups OH, C = O, and CO stretching vibration. The particles collected display spherical morphology with an average of 3.25 nm size by HR-TEM analysis spectra. The photoluminescence showed stability against various excitation wavelengths and had a maximum emission peak at 445 nm. Anti-inflammatory activity is tested using a Bovine serum assay displaying significant results of 87.4 % for SOCC-dots at a concentration of 50 µg/mL due to their superior biocompatibility and water solubility. The results of the cytotoxicity evaluation showed an 85 % survival rate among nauplii after 48 h at concentrations of 80 µg/mL, and the antioxidant activity exhibited outstanding performance, achieving an 87 % efficacy rate for SOCC-dots at a concentration of 50 μg/mL

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food and agricultural residues from farming and processing sectors are undeniably substantial contaminants in both water and land bodies, with diverse undesirable effect on the health of organisms [1]. The growing concerns in this field leads us to a conundrum where, an increased usage of resources leads to a deterioration and a simultaneous urge for a safer ecology [2]. However, recently there has been a growing interest among researchers in the green synthesis of nanomaterials. This greener approach is considered simple, rapid, efficient and eco-friendly [3–5]. The abundance of natural resources has prompted the investigation by using biomass residue as a base for developing novel nanoparticles which becomes the primary notion of this research. One innovative technique to repurpose the biomass residue is to use it as a precursor in synthesizing C-dots. Recently, an acquisition of innovative C-dots from greener sources has shown excellent promises in fields like bioimaging, optoelectronics, drug delivery and sensing applications. The prevailing methods for producing C-dots include expensive equipment such as laser beam, microwave, use of chemicals as precursors, multiple steps of operation and surface passivation to enhance water solubility and photoluminescence. We explore a simple and economical technique for producing C-dots from industrial or agricultural leftovers. Using waste material as precursor minimizes raw material costs and leads to an environmental friendly approach [6,7]. It is important to consider the composition and modification of the C-dots, as these factors can affect their stability, toxicity and solubility [8]. Soybean Oil Cake (SOC) is the agricultural residue obtained after extracting oil from soybean seeds. All of the de-oiled cakes contained 49–51 % hemicellulose, 15–20 % cellulose, 7 % extractives and 25 % lignin [6]. Incorporating oilseed cake as a raw material for the synthesis of C-dots reduces both environmental impact and production cost [6]. C-dots consist of crystalline, quasi-spherical fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with a diameter ranging from 1 nm to 10 nm. They exhibit a moderate quantum confinement effect (QCE) with a bandgap being influenced by the size of the C-dots [9]. Nano-scaled carbon particles have a graphitic core with a functional group on the surface, [10,11]. C-dots are synthesized from molecular carbon precursors utilizing several processes. These processes include pyrolysis, which involves heating at high temperatures in an inert atmosphere, laser ablation conversion of laser beams into carbon-based nanomaterials and microwave synthesis [12].In hydrothermal synthesis, water is a green solvent that is essential for encouraging atom-efficient reactions [13]. Current investigation attempts to enhance the physiochemical properties and biomedical effects of C-dots using cost-effective biomass residue and methodologies leading to an impetus work. Using biomass residues as precursors for the synthesis of C-dots is an economical and sustainable method that improves both economic efficiency and the environment [14–16].

Globally, thousands of fatalities lead to infections giving rise to pathogen resistance in antibiotics as a major issue for the public. To bridge the gap caused by insufficient research and development, researchers are focusing on emphasizing the biomedical applications of C-dots including anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidant, and antibacterial properties. To address the ever-changing antibiotic resistance to foreign sources, it is crucial to find a new and effective approach [17–19]. Consequently, the connotation and demand of evolving a substitute like C-dots are encouraging resources for biomedical applications owing to their biocompatibility, low-toxicity and biodegradability [20].

Our findings reveal the development of a highly sustainable, economically viable and environmentally benign method for producing C-dots from SOC through hydrothermal processing, providing a greener alternative for their synthesis. Moreover, our study extended to examining the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant capabilities of the synthesized SOCC-dots, while their cytotoxicity was rigorously assessed through the brine shrimp lethality assay, a standard protocol for determining biological safety.

As shown in Table 1 . a review of the literature reveals that SOC has been underexplored as a precursor for C-dots compared to other well-defined precursors. Notably, the potential biomedical applications of SOC-derived C-dots have received scant attention. However, SOCC-dots offers promising characteristics, including physical stability, homogeneity, and a high surface-to-volume ratio, as evidenced by its favourable average particle size. Building on these advantages, this study focuses on the biomedical applications of SOCC-dots, leveraging their exceptional optical properties, biocompatibility, and water solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Types of C-dots reported for various synthesis methods
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of various synthesis techniques was examined as shown in Fig. 1 . Various methods, antecedents, and C-dots structures are represented in the research publications that this article summarizes. The hydrothermal method is, astonishingly, the most often used technique for producing C-dots. The widespread adoption of this method is likely attributed to its straightforward operating conditions, low energy requirements, and cost-effective equipment, as evidenced by multiple surveys [30,31]. Conversely, the electrochemical method is the least favoured approach, primarily due to its time-consuming purification steps and restricted precursor availability [32]. Additionally, the data indicates that the chemical oxidation approach is more likely to produce crystalline C-dots followed by hydrothermal method. The synthesis of C-dots is greatly influenced by a number of parameters, including precursors, temperature, synthesis time, technique and pH, according to several studies [33,34].Therefore, in order to develop optimal control conditions for generating C-dots with consistent features, statistical analysis of these parameters is essential.

All the solvents and reagents were bought from Sigma Aldrich (India) are of analytical grade. No additional purification was carried out. Soybean Oil cake was obtained from an agricultural land in Chennai, Tamil Nadu.

A single-step hydrothermal process was used to create the C-dots with slight modifications using SOC as the precursor and polyethylene glycol (PEG-4000) serving as the capping agent. SOC was washed with double distilled water several times and dried in hot air oven at 80 °C. The dried SOC was then crushed into a fine powder and stored for further synthesis steps. Briefly, 5 g of SOC was dispersed in 40 ml of de-ionized water, followed by 1 g of PEG-4000 which was dispersed in 10 ml of de-ionized water. The concoction was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and then maintained at 180 °C for 4 h After naturally cooling to ambient temperature, the resultants underwent centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min and the insoluble contaminants were removed from the supernatant by filtering it via BIOSHARP membrane filters (0.22 µm) and the clear solution underwent dialysis (Mw = 500 Da) for 48 h to remove any remaining unreacted materials. The systematic synthesis of C-dots is illustrated in Fig. 2 . and further stored at 4 °C for further studies and applications [35].

HITACHI U-2900 spectrophotometer was used to analyze the UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-dots. Flurolog HORIBA PL spectrometer was used to record the C-dots emission spectra. HR-TEM images were examined using JEOL Japan, JEM-2100 plus which runs at an accelerated voltage of 200 Kv, while FTIR spectra were recorded on a SHIMADZU, IRTRACER 100. X-ray diffraction analysis was performed on a BRUKER USA D8 Advance, Davinci and Zeta potential analysis was carried out on a Malvern/Nano ZS-90 zeta sizer. A laser wavelength excitation of 532 nm was used to record the results of the Raman spectra investigation carried out with the LabRam HR 800 Raman spectrometer.

The integrated PL intensities and the sample absorbance values were compared to quinine sulphate, which has a known quantum yield of 0.54, to calculate the quantum yield (Φ) of the synthesized SOCC-dots. Eq. (1) was utilized to compute the Φ.

The integrated photoluminescence intensities of the sample and the reference are denoted by I and IR, the absorbance values are represented by A and AR, the refractive indices of the sample and reference are denoted by n and nR, respectively, and the quantum yield values are represented by Φ and ΦR [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on photodegradation using crystal violet dye
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic degradation of synthetic crystal violet dye (CV), a prevalent industrial pollutant, was investigated using SOCC-dots under halogen light irradiation. A simplified adsorption-desorption equilibrium method was employed, involving sonication of 5 ml of photoactive C-dots in 100 ml of CV solution (8 ppm) for 10 min, followed by exposure to halogen light. The photo-reacted material was collected in 1 mL aliquots at 10 min intervals, centrifuged, and studied using UV–Vis absorbance to determine CV concentration. The efficiency of the synthesized samples was evaluated and the absorption spectra were plotted (Fig. 12a), revealing absorption peaks at 584 nm, indicative of dye decomposition. The degradation effectiveness was assessed by plotting C/C0 (initial concentration Vs concentration at different times) against time, yielding a degradation efficiency of approximately 40 % for pure C-dots. The increased activity of photocatalysis is ascribed to the active surface area and reduced size effect, facilitating improved diffusion. Degradation efficiency was calculated using the following formula:

Where, Co and C t are the initial and final concentrations of dye, respectively.

Protein denaturation is a well-implicated source for cellular inflammation [37]. Bovine serum assay (BSA) was utilized as a reactant for the experiment. BSA which makes almost 60 % of the total protein concentration in an animal serum, is frequently used in cell cultures. When exposed to heat, BSA denaturates and generates antigens associated with Type III hypersensitivity reactions, which are related to illnesses such as rheumatoid arthritis and serum sickness [38].

To perform BSA, 3 mL of BSA were taken separately in each falcon tube. 10–50 µg/mL of SOCC-dots was incorporated into the reaction mixture. Control was also prepared similarly without the addition of SOCC-dots. The pH was adjusted to 6.8. The control and sample tubes were incubated at 37 °C for 20 min, followed by at 72 °C for 5 min. The absorbance at 660 nm was determined after letting the sample and control cool for 10 min. Denaturation inhibition was computed and compared for both the samples [39]. The percentage of protein denaturation was computed utilizing the formula (3)

The brine shrimp lethality assay was also used to assess the cytotoxicity of SOCC-dots. Initially, two grams of iodine-free salt was dissolved in 200 ml of de-ionized water. 10 to 12 mL of saltwater were filled into six well ELISA plates, and ten nauplii were gradually introduced to each well plates (5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, and control) at different concentrations along with the addition of the SOCC-dots in various concentrations. ELISA plates were inspected periodically to count the number of live nauplii after the plates were in an incubator for 48 h [40]. The percentage of mortality was calculated using the formula (4)

The antioxidant activity was also evaluated using the scavenging hydroxyl radicals test [41]. SOCC-dots were added in varying concentrations (10–50 μg/mL) to test tubes, which were then exposed to 50 μL of 5 mM H2O2 and ascorbic acid served as the standard. A blank control was prepared by adding 5 mM H2O2 to 50 μL of water without the addition of sample. The test tubes were incubated for >20 min at ambient temperature and the absorbance was measured at 660 nm. The percentage suppression of H2O2 radical generation was computed using Eq. (5)

HR-TEM investigation offers insightful information into the shape and morphology of C-dots. The observed shape, size and structural aspects aid in understanding the unique qualities of the synthesized C-dots. Fig. 3 . depicts the morphology and nanostructure of the prepared C-dots sample. HR-TEM analysis shows that C-dot particles displayed a spherical morphology with an average particle size of 3.25 nm. The spherical form of the C-dots particles indicates that they are structurally stable, homogenous, have a high ratio of surface-to-volume and can effectively distribute among various solutions. Furthermore, this shape has the potential for unique optical features. The C-dots spherical structure improves stability and dispersibility in any mediums, and interacts with the environment [42–44]. The micrograph images reveal the C-dots structure with a lattice-fringe of 0.21 nm. The lattice fringes represent the nanoscale and the configuration of the obtained C-dots [45,46]. These findings suggest potentially specialized uses and emphasizes the importance of the C-dots structure in influencing applications.

The C-dots absorption spectra display two distinct peaks as in Fig. 4 . A strong peak at 275 nm corresponding to sp2 hybridized carbon domains and a minor shoulder peak at 379 nm. These peaks correspond to the π- π* transition of C = C and n-π* transition of C = O bonds respectively [47]. These two absorption bands suggest that C-dots possess both conjugated structures and oxygen (O2) containing functional groups. The data also indicate that there are fewer functional groups present on surface of the C-dots. This results in a larger band gap due to surface passivation [48]. Based on the calculations, the band gap was determined to be 4.4 eV, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 3. Tauc's plot was used to calculate the energy band gap Eg value from the following Eq. (6)
(6)
α h ν = A
(
h ν −
E g
)
n
where hν the photon energy, α is the absorption coefficient, n equals ½ for direct allowed transition and Eg is the optical band gap [49]. The existence of π-conjugation in C-dots molecules results in a much lower band gap than the molecular fundamental gap [50].

The level of oxidation on the surface and the amount of surface defects rise with C-dots, causing a dramatic red shift in their emission-excitation spectra [51]. The PL peak of C-dots emitted the highest with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm [52] as seen in Fig. 5 . The increase in excitation wavelength from 320 to 400 nm leads to the emission peak at red shift in the direction of the longer wavelength. The PL peak shifts to a longer wavelength when the intensity increases from 380 to 400 nm. The behaviour observed in the C-dots may be attributed due to their random distribution, varying sizes, and the different functional groups present on their surface. The primary cause of this excitation dependence is related to the non-homogeneity arising from the C-dots solution [53].

As can be observed in Fig. 6 , the surface state of C-dots plays a critical role in fluorescence because it comprises a variety of functional groups and levels of surface oxidation that serve as energy-trapping sites. The emission of C-dots, a commonly accepted mechanism for fluorescence, is tightly linked to this surface condition. Electrons are excited to a higher energy level at faulty sites on the surface by light of a certain wavelength that matches the bandgap of the C-dots. The emission eventually shifts to longer wavelengths when the electrons eventually return to their ground state and the energy difference falls within the visible spectrum [54].

According to various research, the number of surface flaws increases in tandem with the degree of surface oxidation. The C-dots exhibit a redshifted emission as a result of these imperfections acting as exciton capture centres [54]. Research carried out by various groups has also shown that oxygen-related surface states are responsible for the redshifts in the emission of C-dots produced by hydrothermal treatment and subsequent purification.

[55,56]. An increase in surface oxygen content causes the band gap to narrow, which causes a red-shifted fluorescence. In a study, Liu et al. synthesized yellow- and green-emissive graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and green-emissive carbon nanodots (CNDs) with comparable size distributions and chemical groups. In contrast to the CNDs, they did notice a shift in the GQDs' emission toward longer wavelengths. The cause of this change was identified as different levels of surface oxidation [57]. This results in a reduction of the band gaps that separate the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface functionalities of carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: FTIR analysis was used to determine the surface functional groups. The FTIR spectra in the range from 4000 to 400 cm-1 to quantify different functional groups as shown in Fig. 7 . The presence of -OH functional group is indicated by a broad absorption peak at 3303 cm−1 and a strong, distinct band at 1637 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C = C/C = O. The weak absorption peak at 1238 cm-1 is caused by a stretching vibration of CO bond. All of the absorption peaks indicate the existence of hydroxyl and carbonyl functionalities on the synthesized C-dots. The acquired results corroborate the existence of surface-level active functional groups, which enhances C-dots hydrophilic property and their improved solubility in water [58].

The XRD pattern of the synthesized C-dots is presented in Fig. 8 , exhibiting a broad peak at 2θ = 20.060, corresponding to the (002) lattice plane (JCPDS No: 82–0505). The Debye- Scherrer Eq. (7) was employed to calculate the average crystalline size of the synthesized SOCC-dots. Using the following parameters: K (geometric factor, 0.9), λ (wavelength), θ (Bragg angle), β (line broadening at half-maximum intensity), and D (average crystalline size). These findings imply that carbon dots have an amorphous behaviour [59]. The amorphous behaviour of C-dots is characterized by the lack of a well-defined crystal structure.
(7)
D = K λ / β cos θ

The zeta potential is an essential factor to determine the surface charge of C-dots in a solution. The Zeta potential of −21.3 mV implies that the C-dots have a negative charged surface, as seen in Fig. 9 . The negative charged surface on C-dots is due to the existence of chemical species and functional groups on their surface, which ionize in solution and accumulate negatively charged particles surrounding them. Surface functional groups of C-dots, such as carbonyl (-C = O) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups, contribute to their negative charged surface. The movement in an electric field in the direction of the positive electrode, producing a negative zeta potential [9]. A positive peak in the zeta potential can also be associated with the stability of the carbon dots in suspension due to increased electrostatic repulsion between the particles. As a result, they are less likely to aggregate and more likely to remain in a stable suspension.

An indispensable technique for determining the structural features of flaws and anomalies in graphene-based materials is Raman spectroscopy. The presence of a sp2 hybridization pattern is shown by the "Graphene band," a peak in the Raman spectra at 1581 cm-1. The hexagonal arrangement of carbon atoms is characterized by this pattern, which is a reliable indicator of materials with a regular amorphous structure. An additional peak, known as the "Defect band," at 1335 cm-1, indicates that there are faults and disorder in the carbon material as shown in Fig. 10 . Carbon atoms displaying a sp3 hybridization are frequently the source of these problems. Graphene structural disorder and defect levels are quantified using the ID/IG ratio [60].The intensity ratio ID/IG, was calculated to be 1.09. This serves as a gauge for the degree of graphitization by showing the existence of flaws or amorphous carbon. Surface imperfections are the cause of the noticeably higher value. These surface flaws are linked to oxygenated groups being present on carbon dot surfaces [60,61].

The photostability of the C-dots was evaluated by exposing them to a 300 W tungsten halogen lamp for 2 h The results, presented in Fig. 11 , show no significant decrease in intensity, indicating remarkable photostability. This stability is attributed to the enhanced amorphous structure and comprehensive carbonization of the C-dots [34]. While the intensity of the C-dots remained relatively stable across a wide pH range (1–7), a slight decrease was observed at acidic and neutral pH values, suggesting the presence of acidic functional groups. In contrast, the intensity decreased steadily at basic pH values (8–14). Importantly, the C-dots demonstrated exceptional stability in aqueous solutions across a broad pH range, underscoring their potential for various applications [62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of quantum yield using various precursors of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: This study presents a comparative analysis of the quantum yield of various C-dot precursors synthesized through different methods, as summarized in Table 2 . Initially, hydrothermal treatment of water hyacinth at 180 °C yielded C-dots with a quantum yield of 3.3 %, suitable for Fe3+ sensing applications [63]. Similarly, hydrothermal treatment of cassava stem at 220 °C produced C-dots with a quantum yield of 1.6 %, showing promise for Fe3+ sensing applications [64]. Similarly, sugarcane bagasse underwent hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C, yielding carbon dots with a quantum yield of 9.1 %, effective for Hg2+ sensing [65]. Strawberry juice as a precursor undergoes hydrothermal treatment at 120 °C with a quantum yield of 6.3 %, suitable for Hg2+ sensing [66]. Additionally, waste carbon paper was hydrothermally treated to produce C-dots with a quantum yield of 5.1 %, suitable for TNT sensing [67]. Furthermore, expired passion fruit shell was hydrothermally treated to yield C-dots with a quantum yield of 1.8 % [68]. When hydrothermally treated at temperatures between 150 °C and 200 °C, grass produced carbon dots with quantum yield of 2.5 to 6.2 % were useful for Cu2+ sensing [69]. Waste acorn cups when treated hydrothermally produces quantum yield of 7.38 % [70]. The hydrothermal treatment of Salvia hispanica L. seeds at 180 °C for 4 h resulted in a quantum yield of 17.8 % useful for Cu2+ sensing [69]. Waste acorn cups when treated hydrothermally produces quantum yield of 7.38 % [70]. The hydrothermal treatment of Salvia hispanica L. seeds at 180 °C for 4 h resulted in a quantum yield of 17.8 % [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of decomposition of CV via SOCC-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation of CV dye using SOCC-dots photocatalyst and visible light irradiation was shown in Fig. 12 (a) and (b-c) are the plots of (C/Co) and – ln (C/C0) against irradiation time for SOCC-dots. Digital photograph and chemical structure of CV dye are shown in Fig 13 (a and b). Excitons are produced on the photocatalyst's surface when it comes into contact with halogen light, both in the dye solution and on the catalyst itself. When high-energy photons (hv > Eg) are absorbed by electrons in the valence band and excited to the conduction band, the electron hole partitions. The breakdown mechanism that has been suggested entails the production of photogenerated holes and electrons when light is applied to the solution mixture. While the photogenerated electrons combine with surface oxygen molecules to form superoxide radicals (O2 •−), which are subsequently reduced to hydroperoxyl radicals, the photogenerated holes oxidize surface hydroxyl ions to hydroxyl radicals (OH.) These extremely oxidizing hydroperoxyl radicals produce hydrogen peroxide and OH. The organic pollutant CV dye and reactive oxygen species are first adsorbed onto the catalytic surface during the photocatalytic reaction. The oxidizing species produced during the reaction process oxidize intermediate compounds in several phases until they are mineralized into inorganic acids, water, and carbon dioxide. According to a plethora of research studies, pure C-dots typically cause exciton pairs to recombine more quickly, which minimizes the breakdown of hazardous dyes.

The C-dots exhibited dose-dependent anti-inflammatory effects, with results closely mirroring those of conventional medication, as depicted in Fig. 14 . The anti-inflammatory effects of the standard drug at concentrations of 10–50 µg/mL were 51.1 %, 56.9 %, 66.5 %, 79.8 %, and 89.8 %. Notably, the SOCC-dots demonstrated the highest anti-inflammatory activity at a concentration of 50 µg/mL, with a remarkable 87.4 % efficacy, comparable to the 89.8 % efficacy of the standard drug. Following rigorous analysis, the SOCC-dots show promise as a potential replacement for chemically synthesized anti-inflammatory drugs in the treatment of inflammatory diseases [72]. The C-dots' ability to bind native proteins via hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces helps maintain protein scaffold and prevent denaturation due to heat stress, potentially contributing to the observed suppression of protein denaturation in vitro, as shown in Fig. 15 . Furthermore, the protein denaturation assay provides an early indication of the C-dots' anti-inflammatory capabilities [73].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-inflammatory mechanism of action of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Inflammation is a physiological response undertaken by the body in order to eliminate the underlying source of harm to the body and initiate the subsequent healing mechanism. Inflammation can be triggered by various factors and is linked to several diseases. Consequently, extensive research is conducted to identify potent anti-inflammatory compounds that can effectively alleviate inflammation while minimizing adverse effects. The anti-inflammatory action mechanism of C-dots entails electrostatic interactions between the charged functional groups on C-dots and biomolecules in serum. The charged particles in the C-dots and BSA gets attracted which facilitates the binding affinity and structural orientation of molecules. Conversely, the hydrophilic property and hydroxyl groups of C-dots boosts the electrostatic interaction behaviour of polar and charged biomolecules in serum. Furthermore, hydroxyl groups on C-dots can participate in hydrogen bonding with polar groups in biomolecules, contributing to the stability of the C-dots-biomolecule complex. The surface of C-dots serves as a substrate for the adsorption of biomolecules from serum, driven by a combination of noncovalent interactions, entropic factors, and biomolecule concentration. The small size of C-dots (3.25 nm) provides a large surface area-to-volume ratio, enhancing adsorption and facilitating their anti-inflammatory effects.
The linear regression between anti-inflammatory and C-dots is illustrated in Table 3 .

The linear regression analysis reveals a strong positive linear correlation between both the standard and carbon dot concentrations, indicating a higher baseline anti-inflammatory activity, as illustrated in Fig. 16 . This suggests that increasing concentrations of both the standard and carbon dots are associated with enhanced anti-inflammatory effects.
Anti-inflammatory properties of C-dots is compared with the previous reports as illustrated in Table 4 .

There is no quick and simple screening procedure for cytotoxicity tests, which often proves to be expensive and time-consuming. In modern times, the use of brine shrimp mortality assays is widespread in applied toxicology and research. When it comes to toxicological testing, the Artemia salina assay is frequently utilized for screening a large number of extracts from medicinal plants in search of potential medicinal compounds. The advantage of this assay is that it does not require aseptic procedures, making it a more ethical alternative to the MTT assay, which relies on animal serum. This assay has been increasingly adopted by several labs as an initial step in estimating toxicity. Artemia is an extremely valuable test organism. To assess the cytotoxicity of various types of NPs, we decided to compare the Artemia salina test to the MTT assay due to its speed, ease of use, and affordability. The brine shrimp lethality test is illustrated in Fig. 17 . is employed for testing the cytotoxic potential as it provides information on the cytotoxic effects of the bioactive chemicals on cells. As illustrated in Fig. 18 . the survivability of the live nauplii was tested using various concentrations of SOCC-Dots.

All nauplii (100 %) survived for 24 h across various concentrations of SOCC-Dots, including 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 40 μg/mL, and 80 μg/mL, as well as the control group. However, a concentration-dependent decrease in survival was observed after 48 h, with 90 % and 85 % survival rates at 40 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL SOCC-Dots, respectively. These results suggest a direct correlation between lethality and SOCC-Dots concentration. Notably, the synthesized SOCC-Dots demonstrated minimal toxicity, establishing them as a favorable and potent biological agent for biomedical research applications [77].

The hydrogen peroxide scavenging assay was employed to assess the antioxidant properties of synthesized SOCC-dots, with ascorbic acid serving as the comparative standard. The antioxidant activity of SOCC-dots was evaluated at concentrations spanning 10–50 μg/mL, as illustrated in Fig. 19 . The results demonstrated that SOCC-dots exhibited concentration-dependent antioxidant activity, with inhibition rates ranging from 49 % to 87 % at 10–50 μg/mL, respectively. The highest antioxidant activity of 87 % was achieved at 50 μg/mL, approaching the 90 % inhibition observed with ascorbic acid. The radical scavenging capacity of SOCC-dots is attributed to the presence of oxygenated carbon groups and hydroxyl (OH-) groups, which facilitate the transfer of hydrogen atoms and the breakdown of H2O2, as verified by FT-IR analysis. These findings indicate that SOCC-dots possess significant antioxidant potential, making them a promising candidate for further exploration as an antioxidant agent [75].

Carbon nanomaterials exhibit a unique dual functionality in response to reactive species, capable of acting as either scavengers to mitigate free radicals and ROS or generators of reactive species upon irradiation. This distinctive property sets carbon nanoparticles apart from traditional single-molecule antioxidants like ascorbic acid. Notably, carbon dots (C-dots) can exhibit both antioxidant and oxidative reactions, a phenomenon attributed to their synthesis and processing mechanisms rather than an inherent property of C-dots. The electronic configuration of oxygen, with unpaired electrons in its outer valence shell, renders it highly reactive and prone to the formation of reactive species. ROS encompass both non-free radicals, such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and singlet oxygen (1O2), and free radical oxygen intermediates, including hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide anions (O2 •−) [78].

The reduction of molecular oxygen creates (O2 •−), which are very reactive.
(8)

The superoxide anion dismutase produces molecular oxygen and hydrogen peroxide: Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and hydroxide ions (HO−) are created during partial reduction of H2O2, and H2O is formed with complete reduction.
(9) H2O2 + e − → HO− +•OH
(10) 2 H + + 2 e − + H2O2→ 2 H2O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Understanding the generation of reactive oxygen species in C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the physical and chemical principles of ROS as illustrated in Fig. 20 . The formation of C-dots is crucial for precisely designing functional characteristics. One of the most difficult aspects is determining the exact structure, which is made up of the core and functional groupings. Most research has focused on the most common ROS, including hydroxyl radicals, singlet oxygen, and superanion radicals. C-dots generate radicals based on their structure, which is highly particular. As a result, only in very few circumstances does a single C-dot produce all of the different types of ROS. Radical generation pathways differ, necessitating intentional design and synthesis of the C-dots [78].
The linear regression between anti-oxidant and C-dots is illustrated in Table 5 .

The linear regression analysis demonstrates a significant positive linear relationship between the concentrations of both the standard and carbon dots, implying a robust baseline antioxidant activity, as depicted in Fig. 21 . This finding indicates that higher concentrations of both the standard and carbon dots are linked to increased antioxidant potency.
Anti-oxidant properties of C-dots are compared with the previous reports as illustrated in Table 6 .

In summary, this study successfully employed an environmentally benign hydrothermal process to synthesize C-dots from SOC. A comprehensive range of analytical techniques was utilized to characterize the C-dots, confirming their morphology and distinct functional groups. Photoluminescence excitation experiments provided insights into the electronic transitions within the C-dots, while HR-TEM visually confirmed their spherical structure. These findings demonstrate the viability of using soybean oil cake as a sustainable source for C-dots synthesis, offering new opportunities for resource efficiency.The photocatalytic degradation of crystal violet dye yielded an efficiency of approximately 40 %. Moreover, this study's investigation of the C-dots' optical properties has expanded their potential applications in biomedical fields, including anti-inflammatory, cytotoxic, and antioxidant capabilities.
